topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was deafening, a thick, suffocating blanket. I stared at the roster taped to the wall, my vision blurring at the edges. They had picked him. Not me. The names I’d bled for, the hours I’d sacrificed, the sheer, relentless grind—all dismissed in favor of some other contender. A sharp, hot pressure built behind my eyes. I slammed my gym bag onto the bench, the thud echoing like a gunshot. "Unbelievable," I muttered, the sound ragged and low. My knuckles were white where I gripped the metal frame of my locker. I didn't bother packing my gear; I just walked out, the cool air hitting my face like a slap, each step fueled by a simmering, corrosive frustration that burned deep in my chest.

[story 2]

"This is a joke," I whispered to the empty hallway, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead mocking my disbelief. I replayed the final drills in my head—the perfect execution, the undeniable talent. Yet, here I was, outside the designated meeting room, watching the team load up, their easy camaraderie a cruel counterpoint to my isolation. A tremor ran through my hands; I clenched them until my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. I wanted to scream at the unfairness of it all, to shake the very structure of this decision. I took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my heart, but the energy inside just coiled tighter, a spring wound to breaking point.

[story 3]

The rejection letter felt like ash in my hand. I crumpled the paper slowly, deliberately, watching the edges fray. Every word they wrote felt like a deliberate slight, a dismissal of years spent chasing this singular goal. I paced the length of my small apartment, the floorboards groaning under my restless energy. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights. My jaw was tight, muscles corded beneath my skin. A fierce, almost painful tightening gripped my throat. I let out a sound—a short, sharp expulsion of air—and turned away from the glass, the sheer intensity of my disappointment making my whole body feel too large, too volatile.
